qa_pairs.question: When installing a Python package from a remote Git repository using `pip`, how can one ensure a reproducible installation, and why is this important?
qa_pairs.answer: To ensure a reproducible installation when installing a Python package from a remote Git repository, it is crucial to specify a fixed reference such as a tag (e.g., `v1.0`), a specific commit identifier (e.g., `da39a3ee5e6b4b0d3255bfef95601890afd80709`), or another stable reference in the `pip install` command (e.g., `pip install git+https://git.example.com/MyProject.git@v1.0`). Using the `HEAD` of the repository is not recommended because authors may make changes, which could introduce issues and make your installation non-reproducible over time. Tags and releases on GitHub can typically be found under the 'Releases' section.

qa_pairs.question: Under what circumstances would a user want to create a local Python wheel for a package, and what are the general steps to do so?
qa_pairs.answer: Users may want to create a local Python wheel when a package is only available from a remote repository without any versioning (tags or releases), or if they need to modify its source code. To create a local wheel from a remote repository, one would activate a virtual environment and use the command `pip wheel --no-deps -w $HOME git+https://git.example.com/MyProject.git@<commit id>`, which clones and checks out the repository at the specified reference and builds a wheel in the `$HOME` directory. If source code modifications are necessary, the process involves first cloning the repository, navigating into it, checking out a specific commit, making the required edits, and then running `pip wheel --no-deps -w $HOME .` to create the local wheel from the modified source.
